result:
Most writing advice is wrong for most writers.

That’s a strange place to start, but it’s true in a quiet, boring way that people don’t like to talk about. We like maxims: “show, don’t tell,” “write every day,” “kill your darlings.” They’re comforting. They feel like knobs you can turn on a machine. But writing isn’t a machine; it’s closer to a metabolism. What helps one body can wreck another.

Consider “write every day.” For some people, this is liberation: the ritual dissolves perfectionism. Ten bad sentences a day keep the fear away. For others, it becomes a small, private failure ritual. They miss a day, then two, then feel like they’ve “broken the streak,” so the whole thing collapses under shame. Same sentence. Two opposite effects.

Or “show, don’t tell.” Sometimes you do need to show—let the reader see the trembling hand rather than stating “he was nervous.” But try to “show” everything and your story turns into an exhausting catalog of gestures and scenery. You end up describing the stubborn glint of late-afternoon sunlight in the kitchen blinds when all the reader actually needs is: “She was too tired to cook.”

There’s a pattern here: advice is usually describing one person’s cure for their own, specific problem. They generalize their antidote into a law.

The problem is that we do this everywhere, not just in writing. Productivity advice from extreme morning people gets handed to insomniac night owls. Fitness advice from 25-year-old athletes is handed to 50-year-olds with joint issues. Stoic philosophy, minimalism, entrepreneurship—everywhere, people export their personal medicine as if it’s universal nutrition.

A better question to ask, whenever you encounter a rule, is: “What problem was this invented to solve?” If you don’t have that problem, the rule might be useless or even harmful.

“Write every day” was invented to fight procrastination and the terror of the blank page. If your real problem is that you overwrite and never revise, this rule might keep you generating drafts you’ll never shape. You might need the opposite rule: “Revise something old before you write something new.”

“Show, don’t tell” was invented to fight flat, abstract prose. If your real problem is that scenes drag on forever and you bury key information in atmospheric detail, you might need: “Tell more aggressively. Summarize what doesn’t matter.”

Most people skip the “what problem is this for?” step because it requires an uncomfortable admission: you have to name your own weaknesses. That’s much harder than adopting someone else’s strengths as an aesthetic.

It’s the same with reading. Some writers are told, “read everything.” Others hear, “don’t read while drafting; it will contaminate your voice.” Those sound contradictory until you look at the underlying problems.

If you’re derivative and easily swayed, maybe you need periods of isolation. If you’re stuck in a tiny, repetitive style-cul-de-sac, you probably need to drown in other people’s voices to realize you’re allowed to sound different.

Most people gravitate to the advice that flatters them. The introvert who fears sharing their work loves advice that emphasizes solitude and private discipline. The extrovert who hates revision and adores brainstorming loves advice that says “find a community, workshop constantly.” Both can end up doubling down on their most comfortable tendencies and calling it growth.

I don’t think the real skill is following good advice. The real skill is diagnosis.

That looks less glamorous than “being inspired,” but it’s more important. It means periodically asking yourself:

- Where, exactly, do my projects tend to die—start, middle, or end?
- What do I avoid doing, even when I have time?
- What kind of work leaves me quietly energized rather than depleted?

If you notice your projects always die at 60%, you probably don’t have a starting problem. You have a finishing problem. You might not need more motivation or bigger dreams; you might need a boring, mechanical checklist for the last phase: “print it out, cut 10%, fix the three biggest flaws, ship it even if it hurts.”

If you notice you never revise, advice about “unlocking creativity” won’t help you. Your bottleneck isn’t creativity; it’s confrontation. Revision is a confrontation with the gap between what you imagined and what you made. That’s a different kind of courage than the courage to start.

And there’s another layer under that: some people are helped by gentleness, others by pressure. The same sentence—“no one is forcing you; you can write as slowly as you want”—can be either a relief that unlocks work or a permission slip to drift indefinitely.

So it isn’t just “what advice,” but “what’s your current state” and “how do you respond to pressure vs. permission?” That combination is messy and personal. It doesn’t make for viral slogans.

But it’s closer to how things actually change.

If there’s a single piece of advice I’d risk generalizing, it’s this: treat any rule as a temporary experiment, not an identity.

Try “write every day” for three weeks. Don’t decide if it’s your religion; decide if it solved the problem you actually had. If it didn’t, throw it away without guilt. You’re not betraying some sacred craft tradition; you’re just not the person that advice was invented for.

You’re allowed to be a slow, sporadic writer who finishes a few excellent things. You’re allowed to be a fast, messy writer who relies on brutal editing. You’re allowed to be someone who outlines obsessively or someone who only discovers what they think by typing into the dark.

What matters is not whether you’re following the right rules. What matters is whether, over time, you are making more of the things you actually care about, with a process that doesn’t quietly destroy you.

Everything else—rituals, schedules, aesthetics, tools—is negotiable.

And you’re allowed to renegotiate as often as you change.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
